subtopics: Integer arithmetic and order of operations

glossary.definition: A whole number, either positive, negative, or zero; an element of the set {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...}.
A natural number greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself.
The largest positive integer that divides each of the given integers without remainder.
The smallest positive integer that is divisible by each of the given integers.
A number expressible as p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
For a real number x, |x| = x if x ≥ 0 and |x| = -x if x < 0; the distance from x to 0 on the number line.
An equation stating that two ratios are equal: a/b = c/d.
In the expression a^n, the exponent n indicates the number of times the base a is multiplied by itself.

A differentiable function whose derivative keeps a constant sign on an interval is strictly monotonic there and has AT MOST one root in it; the Intermediate Value Theorem (a sign change across endpoints) confirms exactly one. e.g. f(x)=e^x+x-2 has f'(x)=e^x+1>0 everywhere -> strictly increasing -> exactly one real root total; f(0)=-1<0, f(1)=e-1>0 -> that root lies in (0,1).

canon.name: Distributive law
canon.form: a(b + c) = ab + ac

canon.name: Order of operations (PEMDAS)
canon.form: Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication/Division (left to right) → Addition/Subtraction (left to right)

canon.name: Properties of absolute value
canon.form: |ab| = |a||b|; |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| (triangle inequality)

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic
canon.form: Every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be written uniquely as a product of primes (up to ordering).

canon.name: Euclidean algorithm
canon.form: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b); repeat until remainder is 0.

canon.name: Percent formula
canon.form: part = (percent/100) × whole

glossary.definition: A finite sum of terms of the form a_n x^n where n is a non-negative integer and a_n are coefficients.
The highest power of the variable appearing with a nonzero coefficient.
A value c such that p(c) = 0.
For ax² + bx + c, the discriminant is Δ = b² - 4ac; its sign determines the nature of the roots.
A number of the form a + bi where a, b ∈ ℝ and i = √(-1).
The complex conjugate of a + bi is a - bi; used to rationalize denominators and compute moduli.
The number of times a root r appears as a factor; r has multiplicity k if (x - r)^k | p(x) but (x - r)^(k+1) does not.
Relations between coefficients of a polynomial and symmetric functions of its roots; for x² + bx + c, the sum of roots is -b and product is c.

canon.name: Factor theorem
canon.form: (x - c) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(c) = 0.

canon.name: Remainder theorem
canon.form: The remainder when p(x) is divided by (x - c) equals p(c).

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of algebra
canon.form: Every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root; equivalently, a degree-n polynomial has exactly n roots in ℂ (counted with multiplicity).

canon.name: Binomial theorem
canon.form: (a + b)^n = Σ_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k) a^(n-k) b^k

canon.name: Difference of squares
canon.form: a² - b² = (a - b)(a + b)

canon.name: Sum/difference of cubes
canon.form: a³ ± b³ = (a ± b)(a² ∓ ab + b²)

canon.name: Rational root theorem
canon.form: If p/q is a rational root of a_n x^n + ... + a_0 (in lowest terms), then p | a_0 and q | a_n.

subtopics: Euclidean plane geometry: triangles, circles, polygons
Circles: arcs, chords, and inscribed angles
Solid geometry: volumes and surface areas

glossary.definition: Two geometric figures are congruent if one can be transformed into the other by a rigid motion (rotation, reflection, translation).
Two figures are similar if one is a dilation (scaling) of the other; corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional.
The angle subtended at the center of a unit circle by an arc of length 1; π radians = 180 degrees.
The circle of radius 1 centered at the origin; defines sin θ and cos θ as the y- and x-coordinates of the point at angle θ.
A set of three positive integers (a, b, c) satisfying a² + b² = c²; e.g., (3, 4, 5) and (5, 12, 13).
An angle formed by two chords sharing an endpoint on a circle; its measure is half the intercepted arc.
A line touching a circle at exactly one point, perpendicular to the radius at that point.
The angle between two half-planes sharing a common edge (line), measured in a plane perpendicular to that edge.
The curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle of radius r rolling along a line: x=r(t-sin t), y=r(1-cos t); it is the brachistochrone (fastest-descent) and tautochrone curve.

canon.name: Pythagorean theorem
canon.form: a² + b² = c² for right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c.

canon.name: Law of cosines
canon.form: c² = a² + b² - 2ab cos C

canon.name: Law of sines
canon.form: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C = 2R (R = circumradius)

canon.name: Area of triangle
canon.form: A = (1/2)ab sin C = √(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)) (Heron's formula, s = (a+b+c)/2)

canon.name: Fundamental Pythagorean identity
canon.form: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1

canon.name: Angle addition formulas
canon.form: sin(α ± β) = sin α cos β ± cos α sin β; cos(α ± β) = cos α cos β ∓ sin α sin β

canon.name: Double angle formulas
canon.form: sin 2θ = 2 sin θ cos θ; cos 2θ = cos²θ - sin²θ = 1 - 2sin²θ

canon.name: Inscribed angle theorem
canon.form: An inscribed angle is half the central angle subtending the same arc.

canon.name: Thales' theorem
canon.form: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle.

canon.name: Area and circumference of circle
canon.form: A = πr²; C = 2πr

glossary.definition: A rule assigning to each element of a domain exactly one element of a codomain; formally, a subset of the Cartesian product where each input appears exactly once.
A function f is injective if f(a) = f(b) implies a = b; distinct inputs map to distinct outputs.
A function f: A → B is surjective if every element of B is f(a) for some a ∈ A.
A function that is both injective and surjective; establishes a one-to-one correspondence between domain and codomain.
If f is bijective, its inverse f⁻¹ satisfies f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(y)) = y for all x in the domain and y in the codomain.
log_b(x) = y means b^y = x; it is the inverse of exponentiation.
A line that a curve approaches arbitrarily closely but does not reach (horizontal, vertical, or oblique).
A sequence with a constant difference d between consecutive terms: a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d.
A sequence with a constant ratio r between consecutive terms: a_n = a_1 · r^(n-1).

canon.name: Change of base formula
canon.form: log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b)

canon.name: Logarithm laws
canon.form: log(ab) = log a + log b; log(a/b) = log a - log b; log(a^n) = n log a

canon.name: Sum of geometric series
canon.form: S_n = a(1 - r^n)/(1 - r); infinite sum S = a/(1-r) for |r| < 1

canon.name: Euler's formula
canon.form: e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ

canon.name: Standard conic forms
canon.form: Ellipse: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1; Hyperbola: x²/a² - y²/b² = 1; Parabola: y = ax²

canon.name: Composition of inverses
canon.form: (f ∘ f⁻¹)(x) = x = (f⁻¹ ∘ f)(x) whenever defined

canon.name: De Moivre's theorem
canon.form: (cos θ + i sin θ)^n = cos(nθ) + i sin(nθ)

subtopics: Sequences and series: convergence tests, Taylor series

glossary.definition: lim_{x→a} f(x) = L means for every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that 0 < |x - a| < δ implies |f(x) - L| < ε.
f is continuous at a if lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a); continuous on an interval if continuous at each point.
f'(x) = lim_{h→0} (f(x+h) - f(x))/h; the instantaneous rate of change of f at x.
∫_a^b f(x) dx = lim_{||P||→0} Σ f(x_i*) Δx_i; the limit of Riemann sums over partitions P of [a, b].
A function F such that F'(x) = f(x); also called an indefinite integral, written ∫ f(x) dx = F(x) + C.
A point c in the domain of f where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist.
A point where f''changes sign (concavity changes from up to down or vice versa).
The power series Σ_{n=0}^∞ f^(n)(a)/n! · (x-a)^n representing f near x = a, when f is infinitely differentiable.
For a power series Σ a_n x^n, the value R such that the series converges absolutely for |x| < R and diverges for |x| > R.
A function f is differentiable at a point a if the limit f'(a) = lim_{h→0} (f(a+h) - f(a))/h exists; differentiable implies continuous but not vice versa.

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of calculus (Part 1)
canon.form: If F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x) wherever f is continuous.

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of calculus (Part 2)
canon.form: ∫_a^b f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a) where F is any antiderivative of f.

canon.name: Mean value theorem
canon.form: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a).

canon.name: L'Hôpital's rule
canon.form: If lim f/g is 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f/g = lim f'/g' (provided the latter limit exists).

canon.name: Product rule
canon.form: (fg)' = f'g + fg'

canon.name: Integration by parts
canon.form: ∫ u dv = uv - ∫ v du

canon.name: Ratio test for series
canon.form: If lim |a_{n+1}/a_n| = L < 1, the series Σ a_n converges absolutely; if L > 1, it diverges.

canon.name: Taylor's theorem with remainder
canon.form: f(x) = Σ_{k=0}^{n} f^(k)(a)/k! · (x-a)^k + R_n(x), where R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(c)/(n+1)! · (x-a)^(n+1) for some c between a and x.

canon.name: Intermediate value theorem
canon.form: If f is continuous on [a,b] and f(a) < k < f(b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) with f(c) = k.

glossary.definition: ∂f/∂x is the derivative of f with respect to x holding all other variables fixed.
∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z); a vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent of f.
D_u f(p) = ∇f(p) · u; the rate of change of f at p in the direction of unit vector u.
The matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function; its determinant measures the local change of area/volume under a coordinate transformation.
curl F = ∇ × F; measures the infinitesimal rotation of a vector field at each point.
div F = ∇ · F = ∂F₁/∂x + ∂F₂/∂y + ∂F₃/∂z; measures the net outflow of a vector field per unit volume.
A vector field F with curl F = 0 (equivalently, F = ∇f for some scalar potential f); line integrals are path-independent.
A critical point that is neither a local max nor a local min; the second derivative test gives a negative discriminant D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}².

canon.name: Clairaut's theorem (symmetry of mixed partials)
canon.form: If f_{xy} and f_{yx} are continuous near (a, b), then f_{xy}(a,b) = f_{yx}(a,b).

canon.name: Second derivative test (two variables)
canon.form: Let D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - (f_{xy})² at critical point (a,b). If D > 0 and f_{xx} > 0: local min; f_{xx} < 0: local max; D < 0: saddle point.

canon.name: Lagrange multiplier condition
canon.form: At a constrained extremum of f subject to g = 0: ∇f = λ∇g.

canon.name: Change of variables formula
canon.form: ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA = ∫∫_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J(u,v)| du dv where J = ∂(x,y)/∂(u,v).

canon.name: Green's theorem
canon.form: ∮_C (P dx + Q dy) = ∬_D (∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y) dA (C positively oriented boundary of D).

canon.name: Stokes' theorem
canon.form: ∮_C F · dr = ∬_S (curl F) · dS where C = ∂S.

canon.name: Divergence theorem (Gauss)
canon.form: ∯_S F · dS = ∭_V (div F) dV where S = ∂V.

canon.name: Chain rule (multivariable)
canon.form: dz/dt = (∂z/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂z/∂y)(dy/dt)

glossary.definition: A set V with operations of addition and scalar multiplication satisfying 8 axioms (commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identities, inverses); elements are called vectors.
Vectors v₁,...,v_n are linearly independent if c₁v₁ + ... + c_n v_n = 0 implies all c_i = 0.
A linearly independent spanning set for a vector space; the number of elements is the dimension.
The dimension of the column space (or row space) of a matrix; equals the number of pivot columns in its row echelon form.
ker(A) = {x : Ax = 0}; a subspace of the domain whose dimension is the nullity.
A scalar λ such that Av = λv for some nonzero vector v (the corresponding eigenvector).
A nonzero vector v such that Av = λv for some scalar λ (the eigenvalue); the direction is preserved under the linear map A.
det(A - λI); its roots are the eigenvalues of A.
A square matrix Q with Q^T Q = I; its columns form an orthonormal basis; det Q = ±1.
A symmetric matrix A such that x^T Ax > 0 for all nonzero x; equivalently, all eigenvalues are positive.
A square matrix with determinant 0; equivalently, it has no inverse; equivalently, its columns are linearly dependent.
The trace of a square matrix A, denoted tr(A), is the sum of its diagonal entries; tr(A) = sum of eigenvalues (counted with multiplicity).
A function T: V → W between vector spaces satisfying T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v); equivalently, a map preserving the vector space structure.
A scalar det(A) (or |A|) assigned to a square matrix A; it is the signed volume-scaling factor of the linear map, is nonzero iff A is invertible, and is multiplicative: det(AB)=det(A)det(B).
The span of a set of vectors is the set of all their linear combinations; it is the smallest subspace containing them.
The number of vectors in any basis of a vector space, an invariant of the space (e.g. R^n has dimension n).
A square matrix A is diagonalizable if A = P D P^-1 for some diagonal matrix D, equivalently if it has a basis of eigenvectors; D's diagonal holds the eigenvalues.
For an eigenvalue lambda of A, the subspace of all vectors v with A v = lambda v (the kernel of A - lambda I); its dimension is lambda's geometric multiplicity.
A map <u,v> on a vector space that is linear, (conjugate-)symmetric, and positive-definite; it defines length ||v|| = sqrt(<v,v>) and orthogonality <u,v> = 0.

canon.name: Rank-nullity theorem
canon.form: dim(ker T) + dim(im T) = dim(domain); rank(A) + nullity(A) = n for an m×n matrix.

canon.name: Spectral theorem (real symmetric matrices)
canon.form: Every real symmetric matrix A is orthogonally diagonalizable: A = Q Λ Q^T where Q is orthogonal and Λ is diagonal with real eigenvalues.

canon.name: Cayley-Hamilton theorem
canon.form: Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: p(A) = 0.

canon.name: Determinant expansion and properties
canon.form: det(AB) = det(A)det(B); det(A^T) = det(A); det(A⁻¹) = 1/det(A); row-swap negates det.

canon.name: Gram-Schmidt process
canon.form: Given basis {v_i}, define orthonormal basis {e_i} by e_k = (v_k - Σ_{j<k} (v_k · e_j)e_j) / ‖...‖.

canon.name: Diagonalization criterion
canon.form: An n×n matrix A is diagonalizable iff it has n linearly independent eigenvectors; equivalently, the geometric multiplicity equals algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue.

canon.name: Singular value decomposition
canon.form: Any m×n matrix A = UΣV^T where U (m×m), V (n×n) are orthogonal and Σ is m×n diagonal with singular values σ_i ≥ 0.

canon.name: Invertibility equivalences
canon.form: TFAE: A is invertible, det A ≠ 0, rank A = n, Ax=0 has only trivial solution, Ax=b has unique solution for every b.

subtopics: Second-order linear ODEs with constant coefficients
Method of undetermined coefficients and variation of parameters
Systems of ODEs and phase plane analysis
Power series solutions and Frobenius method
Partial differential equations: heat, wave, Laplace equations

glossary.definition: An equation relating a function y(x) and its derivatives; order is the highest derivative present.
An ODE of the form dy/dx = f(x)g(y), solvable by separating variables: ∫ dy/g(y) = ∫ f(x) dx.
A function μ(x) multiplied through a linear ODE dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x) to make the left side an exact derivative; μ(x) = e^(∫P dx).
For ay'' + by' + cy = 0, the algebraic equation ar² + br + c = 0 whose roots determine the general solution.
W(y₁, y₂) = y₁y₂' - y₁'y₂; if W ≠ 0 on an interval, y₁ and y₂ are linearly independent solutions.
L{f(t)} = F(s) = ∫_0^∞ e^(-st) f(t) dt; transforms a differential equation into an algebraic equation in s.
A constant solution y = c where f(c) = 0; stable if nearby solutions approach it, unstable if they diverge.
Representation of a function as a series of eigenfunctions of a differential operator (e.g., Fourier series in terms of sin and cos).

An integral transform sending f(t) to f-hat(omega)=integral of f(t)e^(-i*omega*t) dt, decomposing a function into its frequency content; it turns differentiation and convolution into algebra, central to solving linear ODEs/PDEs.

canon.name: General solution of 2nd-order linear ODE (constant coefficients)
canon.form: Roots r₁ ≠ r₂ real: y = c₁e^(r₁x) + c₂e^(r₂x); repeated root r: y = (c₁ + c₂x)e^(rx); complex roots α ± βi: y = e^(αx)(c₁cos βx + c₂sin βx).

canon.name: Variation of parameters formula
canon.form: y_p = y₁ ∫(-y₂ g/W) dx + y₂ ∫(y₁ g/W) dx where W = Wronskian(y₁, y₂).

canon.name: Existence and uniqueness theorem (Picard-Lindelöf)
canon.form: If f(x,y) and ∂f/∂y are continuous in a rectangle containing (x₀, y₀), then the IVP y' = f(x,y), y(x₀)=y₀ has a unique solution.

canon.name: Convolution theorem
canon.form: L{(f * g)(t)} = F(s)G(s); L⁻¹{F(s)G(s)} = ∫_0^t f(τ)g(t-τ) dτ

canon.name: Heat equation
canon.form: u_t = α² u_{xx}; solved on [0,L] by separation of variables giving u(x,t) = Σ B_n sin(nπx/L) e^(-α²(nπ/L)²t)

canon.name: Wave equation
canon.form: u_{tt} = c² u_{xx}; D'Alembert solution: u(x,t) = (1/2)[f(x+ct)+f(x-ct)] + (1/2c)∫_{x-ct}^{x+ct} g(s) ds

subtopics: Discrete distributions: Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Geometric
Law of large numbers and central limit theorem

glossary.definition: The set Ω of all possible outcomes of a random experiment.
A subset of the sample space; a measurable collection of outcomes to which a probability is assigned.
P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B)/P(B) for P(B) > 0; the probability of A given that B has occurred.
Events A and B are independent if P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B); knowing B gives no information about A.
A measurable function X: Ω → ℝ that assigns a numerical value to each outcome.
E[X] = Σ x P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫ x f(x) dx (continuous); the probability-weighted average value.
Var(X) = E[(X - μ)²] = E[X²] - (E[X])²; measures spread around the mean.
M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]; its k-th derivative at t=0 equals the k-th moment E[X^k].
F(x) = P(X ≤ x); a right-continuous, non-decreasing function from ℝ to [0,1].
A measure P on a sample space Ω satisfying P(Ω)=1 and countable additivity; P(A) ∈ [0,1] represents the likelihood of event A. Axiomatized by Kolmogorov.
A function that describes how probabilities are assigned to the values of a random variable; for discrete RVs: a PMF; for continuous RVs: a PDF integrating to 1.
A random variable whose range is a finite or countably infinite set; described by a probability mass function P(X=x) summing to 1.
A random variable whose range is an uncountable set (e.g. ℝ or an interval); described by a probability density function f(x) with ∫f(x)dx = 1.
P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B); relates the conditional probability P(A|B) to the inverse conditional P(B|A) and the marginal probabilities.
If X₁, X₂, ... are i.i.d. with mean μ and variance σ², then (X̄ₙ - μ)/(σ/√n) converges in distribution to N(0,1) as n → ∞.
The (strong) law of large numbers: the sample mean X̄ₙ converges almost surely to E[X] as n → ∞ (for i.i.d. finite-mean RVs).

canon.name: Bayes' theorem
canon.form: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) / [P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|Aᶜ)P(Aᶜ)]

canon.name: Law of total probability
canon.form: P(B) = Σ_i P(B|A_i)P(A_i) for any partition {A_i} of Ω.

canon.name: Binomial distribution
canon.form: P(X=k) = C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k); E[X]=np; Var(X)=np(1-p)

canon.name: Poisson distribution
canon.form: P(X=k) = e^(-λ)λ^k/k!; E[X]=Var(X)=λ; arises as limit of Binomial as n→∞, p→0, np→λ.

canon.name: Normal distribution
canon.form: f(x) = (1/σ√(2π)) exp(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)); E[X]=μ, Var(X)=σ²

canon.name: Central limit theorem
canon.form: If X₁,...,X_n are i.i.d. with mean μ and variance σ², then (X̄_n - μ)/(σ/√n) → N(0,1) in distribution as n → ∞.

canon.name: Law of large numbers (strong)
canon.form: If X₁, X₂,... are i.i.d. with E[X₁] = μ, then X̄_n = (X₁+...+X_n)/n → μ almost surely.

canon.name: Pigeonhole principle
canon.form: If n+1 objects are placed into n boxes, then at least one box contains two or more objects.

subtopics: Descriptive statistics: mean, median, variance, IQR
Sampling distributions and the standard error

glossary.definition: A population is the entire set of subjects; a sample is a subset drawn from it. Statistics from samples estimate population parameters.
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic; for the sample mean, SE = σ/√n.
An interval [L, U] constructed from data such that it contains the true parameter with a stated probability (confidence level) in repeated sampling.
The default claim being tested; typically a statement of no effect or no difference.
The probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true; small p-values provide evidence against H₀.
Rejecting H₀ when it is true; probability is the significance level α.
Failing to reject H₀ when it is false; power = 1 - β.
r = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y); ranges from -1 to 1; measures the linear association between two variables.
In ŷ = a + bx, the slope b = r · (s_y/s_x) = Σ(x_i - x̄)(y_i - ȳ) / Σ(x_i - x̄)².

canon.name: Z-test statistic
canon.form: z = (X̄ - μ₀) / (σ/√n); used when population σ is known.

canon.name: t-test statistic (one sample)
canon.form: t = (X̄ - μ₀) / (s/√n); follows t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom.

canon.name: Chi-square goodness-of-fit statistic
canon.form: χ² = Σ (O_i - E_i)² / E_i; follows χ²(k-1) distribution under H₀.

canon.name: Least-squares regression line
canon.form: ŷ = ȳ + r(s_y/s_x)(x - x̄); minimizes Σ(y_i - ŷ_i)².

canon.name: 95% confidence interval for mean
canon.form: X̄ ± t*(s/√n) where t* is the critical value for t_{n-1} at 95%.

canon.name: Chebyshev's inequality
canon.form: P(|X - μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k² for any k > 0; distribution-free bound.

canon.name: Neyman-Pearson lemma
canon.form: The most powerful test at level α for H₀: θ=θ₀ vs H₁: θ=θ₁ rejects based on the likelihood ratio L(θ₁)/L(θ₀) > c.

subtopics: Groups: definition, examples (Z, Z_n, S_n, D_n, GL_n)

glossary.definition: A set G with a binary operation · satisfying: (i) closure, (ii) associativity, (iii) existence of identity e with ge = eg = g, (iv) existence of inverses g⁻¹ with gg⁻¹ = e.
A group in which the operation is commutative: ab = ba for all a, b.
|G|, the cardinality of the set G.
The smallest positive integer n such that g^n = e; if no such n exists, the element has infinite order.
A subset H ⊆ G closed under the group operation and inverses, forming a group in its own right. Equivalently: H ≠ ∅ and a,b ∈ H ⟹ ab⁻¹ ∈ H.
A subgroup N ⊴ G such that gNg⁻¹ = N for all g ∈ G; equivalently, left and right cosets coincide.
A left coset of H in G is gH = {gh : h ∈ H}; the cosets partition G into equal-sized subsets.
G/N where N ⊴ G; the set of cosets {gN} with multiplication (aN)(bN) = (ab)N.
A function φ: G → H satisfying φ(ab) = φ(a)φ(b) for all a, b ∈ G.
ker φ = {g ∈ G : φ(g) = e_H}; always a normal subgroup of G.
A nontrivial group with no proper nontrivial normal subgroups.
A group whose order is a power of a prime p; every nontrivial p-group has a nontrivial center.
A group generated by a single element g; every element is a power g^k. Denoted ⟨g⟩. A cyclic group of order n is isomorphic to Z_n.
The group D_n of symmetries of a regular n-gon; has 2n elements (n rotations + n reflections). D_n = ⟨r,s | r^n=s^2=e, srs=r^{-1}⟩.
The group S_n of all permutations of {1,...,n}; has order n!. Every finite group embeds in some S_n (Cayley's theorem).
The subgroup A_n of S_n consisting of all even permutations; has order n!/2. A_n is simple for n≥5.
A bijective group homomorphism φ: G→H such that φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). Two isomorphic groups are structurally identical. Written G≅H.
An isomorphism from a group G to itself. The set of all automorphisms Aut(G) forms a group under composition.
Z(G) = {z∈G : zg=gz for all g∈G}. The set of elements that commute with everything; Z(G) is always a normal subgroup.
For groups G, H: the direct product G×H has elements (g,h) with component-wise multiplication. |G×H|=|G||H|.
If H is a subgroup of finite group G, then |H| divides |G|. The index [G:H]=|G|/|H| is the number of cosets of H in G.
The number of distinct (left) cosets of H in G, written [G:H]=|G|/|H| for finite groups.

For a finite group G with |G|=p^a·m (p prime, p∤m): (1) G has a subgroup of order p^a (Sylow p-subgroup); (2) all Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate; (3) the number n_p of Sylow p-subgroups satisfies n_p≡1(mod p) and n_p | m.
A group whose upper central series terminates at G (equivalently, whose lower central series terminates at {e}). Every p-group is nilpotent. Direct products of nilpotent groups are nilpotent.
A group G with a subnormal series G=G_0▷G_1▷…▷G_k={e} where each quotient G_i/G_{i+1} is abelian. All abelian groups and all nilpotent groups are solvable. S_n is not solvable for n≥5.
A homomorphism φ: G→Sym(X) from group G to the permutation group of set X. Equivalently, a function G×X→X satisfying e·x=x and (gh)·x=g·(h·x).
In a group action G×X→X, the orbit of x∈X is {g·x : g∈G}. The orbits partition X.
In a group action, the stabilizer (isotropy group) of x∈X is Stab(x)={g∈G : g·x=x}. A subgroup of G. Orbit-stabilizer theorem: |Orb(x)||Stab(x)|=|G|.
A group whose elements are permutations (bijections) of a set, with composition as the group operation. Every group is isomorphic to a permutation group (Cayley).
The group F_n generated by n symbols with no relations other than group axioms. Every group is a quotient of a free group.
⟨generators | relations⟩. E.g. Z_n=⟨a | a^n=e⟩; D_n=⟨r,s | r^n=s^2=e, srs^{-1}=r^{-1}⟩.
a and b in group G are conjugate if b=gag^{-1} for some g∈G. Conjugate elements have the same order. Conjugacy classes partition the group.
An automorphism of G of the form φ_g(x)=gxg^{-1} for fixed g∈G. The set of all inner automorphisms Inn(G) is a normal subgroup of Aut(G).
A generalization of direct product: G=N⋊H where N⊴G. When φ: H→Aut(N) is trivial, the semidirect product is a direct product.
A structure-preserving map between algebraic structures (groups, rings, etc.). For groups: f(ab)=f(a)f(b). The kernel is the set of elements mapping to the identity; the image is a substructure.
A bijection from a set to itself. In S_n, represented in cycle notation: (1 2 5 4)(2 3) means 1→2→5→4→1 and 2→3→2. Order of a permutation = LCM of cycle lengths.
In a permutation group, a cycle (a_1 a_2 ... a_k) maps a_1→a_2→...→a_k→a_1. Cycle length determines the order. Permutations decompose uniquely into disjoint cycles.
For a group homomorphism φ: G → H, the image Im(φ) = {φ(g) | g ∈ G} is the set of all outputs; Im(φ) is a subgroup of H.
An element g of a group G is a generator if every element of G can be written as a power (or product of powers) of g; G = ⟨g⟩ means g generates G.
A group G is finitely generated if there exists a finite set S ⊂ G such that every element of G is a product of elements from S and their inverses.
An element g of a group G is a torsion element (or element of finite order) if g^n = e for some positive integer n; the set of all torsion elements forms the torsion subgroup.
The torsion subgroup Tor(G) of an abelian group G is the subgroup of all elements of finite order.
The index [G : H] of a subgroup H in G is the number of distinct (left or right) cosets of H in G; by Lagrange's theorem, [G : H] = |G| / |H| for finite groups.
A group presentation ⟨generators | relations⟩ describes a group by specifying generators and the relations among them; e.g., Z_n = ⟨a | a^n = e⟩.
In a group presentation, a relation is an equation among generators that must hold in the group; e.g., a^n = e, or ab = ba (commutativity relation).
A p-subgroup of G is a subgroup whose order is a power of the prime p; the Sylow p-subgroup is the maximal p-subgroup.
A group G is finitely generated if there exists a finite set S ⊂ G such that every element of G is a product of elements from S and their inverses; equivalently, G = ⟨S⟩ for some finite S.
The set of all elements conjugate to a given g in a group, { x g x^-1 : x in G }; conjugacy classes partition the group and their sizes give the class equation.
The normalizer of a subset S in a group G is N_G(S) = { g in G : g S g^-1 = S }, the largest subgroup of G in which S is normal.
The subgroup generated by all commutators [a,b] = a b a^-1 b^-1; it is the smallest normal subgroup whose quotient (the abelianization) is abelian.
Every group G is isomorphic to a subgroup of the symmetric group on G, via its action on itself by left multiplication.
For a finite group G: |G| = |Z(G)| + sum of [G : C_G(x_i)] over representatives of the non-central conjugacy classes; a key counting tool (e.g. for p-groups).

canon.name: Lagrange's theorem
canon.form: If H is a subgroup of a finite group G, then |H| divides |G|, and the index [G:H] = |G|/|H| equals the number of distinct cosets of H.

canon.name: Corollary: order of elements divides group order
canon.form: The order of any element g ∈ G divides |G|; in particular, g^|G| = e.

canon.name: First isomorphism theorem
canon.form: If φ: G → H is a group homomorphism, then G/ker φ ≅ im φ.

canon.name: Second isomorphism theorem
canon.form: If H ≤ G and N ⊴ G, then H/(H ∩ N) ≅ HN/N.

canon.name: Third isomorphism theorem
canon.form: If N ⊴ K ⊴ G and N ⊴ G, then (G/N)/(K/N) ≅ G/K.

canon.name: Cayley's theorem
canon.form: Every group G is isomorphic to a subgroup of the symmetric group S_{|G|}; each g ∈ G acts by left multiplication.

canon.name: Sylow's first theorem
canon.form: If p^k divides |G|, then G has a subgroup of order p^k.

canon.name: Sylow's second theorem
canon.form: Any two Sylow p-subgroups of G are conjugate.

canon.name: Sylow's third theorem
canon.form: The number n_p of Sylow p-subgroups satisfies n_p ≡ 1 (mod p) and n_p divides |G|/p^k.

canon.name: Orbit-stabilizer theorem
canon.form: |G| = |Orb(x)| · |Stab(x)| for any group action of G on a set X and any x ∈ X.

canon.name: Classification of finite cyclic groups
canon.form: Every cyclic group of order n is isomorphic to Z_n; every subgroup of Z_n is cyclic of order dividing n.

canon.name: Cauchy's theorem
canon.form: If prime p divides |G|, then G has an element of order p.

glossary.definition: A set R with addition and multiplication satisfying: (R,+) is an abelian group, multiplication is associative, and distributive laws hold.
A ring in which multiplication is commutative: ab = ba for all a, b.
An element 1 ∈ R such that 1·a = a·1 = a for all a; a ring with unity is called a unital ring.
A subset I ⊆ R closed under addition and satisfying rI ⊆ I and Ir ⊆ I for all r ∈ R; the kernel of any ring homomorphism is an ideal.
An ideal P ≠ R such that ab ∈ P implies a ∈ P or b ∈ P; R/P is an integral domain iff P is prime.
An ideal M ≠ R with no ideal strictly between M and R; R/M is a field iff M is maximal.
A commutative unital ring with no zero divisors; ab = 0 implies a = 0 or b = 0.
A commutative ring in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse; equivalently, a commutative division ring.
An integral domain in which every nonzero non-unit element factors uniquely into irreducibles (up to order and units).
An integral domain in which every ideal is principal, i.e., generated by a single element.
An element α over a field F is algebraic if it satisfies some nonzero polynomial with coefficients in F; otherwise it is transcendental.
[E:F] = dim_F E; the dimension of E as a vector space over F.
For a ring R, R[x] is the ring of polynomials in x with coefficients in R. If R is an integral domain, so is R[x]. If F is a field, F[x] is a PID.
A field F contained in a larger field E. Written E/F. The degree [E:F]=dim_F(E). Key examples: Q(√2)/Q (degree 2), C/R (degree 2).
A field extension F̄/F where every polynomial in F[x] splits into linear factors. Every field has an algebraic closure (unique up to isomorphism). C is the algebraic closure of R.
The smallest field extension over which a polynomial f∈F[x] factors into linear factors. Unique up to isomorphism.
A non-constant polynomial f∈F[x] that cannot be factored as f=gh with g,h∈F[x] both of lower degree. Analogous to prime numbers.
The smallest positive integer n such that n·1=0 (summing the identity n times). If no such n exists, the characteristic is 0. Finite fields have prime characteristic.
A field with finitely many elements. A finite field with q elements exists iff q=p^n for a prime p; it is unique up to isomorphism, denoted F_q or GF(q).
For a ring R and an ideal I: R/I is the quotient ring with elements {a+I : a∈R} and operations (a+I)+(b+I)=(a+b)+I, (a+I)(b+I)=ab+I.
A function f: R→S between rings preserving +, ×, and the multiplicative identity: f(a+b)=f(a)+f(b), f(ab)=f(a)f(b), f(1_R)=1_S.
An element u∈R is a unit if it has a multiplicative inverse (∃v: uv=vu=1). The set of units R× forms a group under multiplication.
An element a in a ring R such that a^n=0 for some positive integer n. The set of all nilpotent elements forms an ideal (the nilradical).
If m_1,...,m_k are pairwise coprime integers, then Z_{m_1⋯m_k} ≅ Z_{m_1}×⋯×Z_{m_k}. For rings: if I_1∩I_2=I_1I_2, then R/(I_1∩I_2)≅R/I_1×R/I_2.
Studies the correspondence between field extensions E/F and subgroups of the Galois group Gal(E/F). A polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is a solvable group.
Gal(E/F) = the group of field automorphisms of E that fix F pointwise. For Galois extensions [E:F]=|Gal(E/F)|.
An abelian group (V,+) with a scalar multiplication F×V→V satisfying distributivity and compatibility axioms. Dim V = cardinality of any basis.
A subset S of ring R that is itself a ring under the same operations. Must contain 0, be closed under +, −, and ×. Every subring of a commutative ring is commutative.
A subset F' of a field F that is itself a field under the same operations. The smallest subfield of any field is called its prime subfield.
A nonzero element a in a ring R such that ab=0 or ba=0 for some nonzero b. An integral domain is a commutative ring with unity and no zero divisors.
The ring Z[i]={a+bi : a,b∈Z}. A Euclidean domain. The units are ±1, ±i. The Gaussian primes are not the same as ordinary primes.
The characteristic of a ring R is the smallest positive integer n such that n·1 = 0 in R; if no such n exists, the characteristic is 0. For a field, the characteristic is 0 or a prime p.
For an element algebraic over a field, the unique monic irreducible polynomial of least degree having it as a root; it divides every polynomial that annihilates the element.
The smallest field containing an integral domain D, built from formal quotients a/b (b nonzero) of its elements (e.g. Q is the field of fractions of Z).
An element of a field extension that is not algebraic over the base field -- not a root of any nonzero polynomial with base-field coefficients (e.g. pi over Q).

canon.name: First isomorphism theorem for rings
canon.form: If φ: R → S is a ring homomorphism, then R/ker φ ≅ im φ.

canon.name: Correspondence theorem (rings)
canon.form: There is a bijection between ideals of R containing I and ideals of R/I, preserving inclusion, primality, and maximality.

canon.name: Every PID is a UFD
canon.form: In a PID, every nonzero non-unit element factors uniquely into irreducibles; every irreducible is prime.

canon.name: Gauss's lemma
canon.form: The product of primitive polynomials in Z[x] is primitive; consequently Z[x] is a UFD.

canon.name: Eisenstein's irreducibility criterion
canon.form: If f = a_n x^n + ... + a_0 ∈ Z[x] and prime p divides a_0,...,a_{n-1} but not a_n, and p² does not divide a_0, then f is irreducible over Q.

canon.name: Tower law for field extensions
canon.form: [E:F] = [E:K][K:F] for fields F ⊆ K ⊆ E.

canon.name: Characterization of simple extensions
canon.form: F(α) ≅ F[x]/(m_α(x)) where m_α is the minimal polynomial of α; [F(α):F] = deg m_α.

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory
canon.form: For a Galois extension E/F with group G = Gal(E/F): there is an inclusion-reversing bijection between subgroups of G and intermediate fields K (F ⊆ K ⊆ E); [G:H] = [K_H:F] and |H| = [E:K_H].

canon.name: Algebraic closure existence
canon.form: Every field F has an algebraic closure F̄ (algebraically closed and algebraic over F); it is unique up to isomorphism over F.

subtopics: Metric spaces: open, closed, compact, connected sets
Sequences and series of functions: uniform convergence
Differentiation: mean value theorem, Taylor's theorem
Measure theory: σ-algebras, measures, measurable functions

glossary.definition: A metric space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges; the reals are the unique (up to isomorphism) complete ordered field.
A set U in a metric space such that every point has an open ball entirely within U; equivalently, U = int(U).
A set K where every open cover has a finite subcover; in ℝⁿ, equivalently, closed and bounded (Heine-Borel).
f is uniformly continuous on E if for all ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that d(x,y) < δ implies |f(x)-f(y)| < ε, with δ independent of the choice of x.
f_n → f uniformly on E if sup_{x∈E} |f_n(x) - f(x)| → 0; it preserves continuity, and allows interchange of limit and integral.
A collection Σ of subsets of a set X closed under complements and countable unions; provides the domain for a measure.
A countably additive function μ: Σ → [0,∞] on a σ-algebra with μ(∅) = 0.
A set E ⊆ ℝ is Lebesgue measurable if for all ε > 0 there is an open set U ⊇ E with m(U\ E) < ε (outer-regularity characterization).
A property holds almost everywhere if the set of points where it fails has measure zero.
L^p(μ) = {f measurable : ∫|f|^p dμ < ∞} with norm ‖f‖_p = (∫|f|^p dμ)^(1/p); a Banach space for 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞.

canon.name: Completeness of ℝ (least upper bound property)
canon.form: Every nonempty subset of ℝ that is bounded above has a supremum in ℝ.

canon.name: Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem
canon.form: Every bounded sequence in ℝⁿ has a convergent subsequence.

canon.name: Heine-Borel theorem
canon.form: A subset of ℝⁿ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

canon.name: Intermediate value theorem (analysis form)
canon.form: If f: [a,b] → ℝ is continuous and f(a) < 0 < f(b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) with f(c) = 0.

canon.name: Uniform limit theorem
canon.form: If f_n → f uniformly on E and each f_n is continuous, then f is continuous.

canon.name: Weierstrass M-test
canon.form: If |f_n(x)| ≤ M_n for all x and Σ M_n < ∞, then Σ f_n converges uniformly and absolutely.

canon.name: Lebesgue dominated convergence theorem
canon.form: If f_n → f a.e. and |f_n| ≤ g with g integrable, then ∫ f_n → ∫ f.

canon.name: Monotone convergence theorem
canon.form: If 0 ≤ f_n ↑ f a.e., then ∫ f_n → ∫ f (possibly ∞).

canon.name: Fatou's lemma
canon.form: For nonneg. measurable f_n: ∫ liminf f_n ≤ liminf ∫ f_n.

canon.name: Hölder's inequality
canon.form: ∫|fg| dμ ≤ ‖f‖_p ‖g‖_q for 1/p + 1/q = 1 (conjugate exponents).

canon.name: Fubini's theorem
canon.form: For integrable f on a product measure space: ∫∫ f(x,y) d(μ×ν) = ∫(∫ f(x,y) dν) dμ = ∫(∫ f(x,y) dμ) dν.

glossary.definition: A function f: U → ℂ (U open in ℂ) that is complex-differentiable at every point of U; equivalently, satisfies the Cauchy-Riemann equations with continuous partials.
A function representable by a convergent power series in a neighborhood of each point; for complex functions, analyticity and holomorphicity are equivalent.
For f = u + iv: ∂u/∂x = ∂v/∂y and ∂u/∂y = -∂v/∂x; necessary and (with continuous partials) sufficient for holomorphicity.
∫_γ f(z) dz = ∫_a^b f(γ(t))γ'(t) dt for a parameterized curve γ: [a,b] → ℂ.
A point z₀ where f is not defined or not holomorphic, but f is holomorphic in some punctured disk 0 < |z - z₀| < r.
Res(f, z₀) = a₋₁, the coefficient of (z - z₀)⁻¹ in the Laurent expansion of f at z₀; for a simple pole: Res(f, z₀) = lim_{z→z₀} (z-z₀)f(z).
A map z ↦ (az+b)/(cz+d) with ad - bc ≠ 0; a bijection of the Riemann sphere ℂ∪{∞} that maps circles and lines to circles and lines.
A function holomorphic on all of ℂ (no singularities in the finite plane); e.g., e^z, sin z, polynomials.
A function holomorphic on a domain except at isolated poles; locally a ratio of holomorphic functions.

canon.name: Cauchy's integral theorem
canon.form: If f is holomorphic on a simply connected domain D and γ is a closed contour in D, then ∮_γ f(z) dz = 0.

canon.name: Residue theorem
canon.form: ∮_γ f(z) dz = 2πi Σ_k Res(f, z_k) where the sum is over poles z_k enclosed by γ (counted with winding number).

canon.name: Liouville's theorem
canon.form: Every bounded entire function is constant.

canon.name: Fundamental theorem of algebra (complex analysis proof)
canon.form: Every non-constant polynomial has a root in ℂ (follows from Liouville: if p(z) ≠ 0 everywhere, then 1/p is bounded entire, hence constant).

canon.name: Identity theorem
canon.form: If f and g are holomorphic on a connected domain D and f = g on a set with an accumulation point in D, then f = g on all of D.

canon.name: Maximum modulus principle
canon.form: A nonconstant holomorphic function on a domain D cannot attain its maximum modulus on the interior of D.

canon.name: Riemann mapping theorem
canon.form: Any simply connected proper open subset of ℂ is conformally equivalent to the open unit disk.

canon.name: Laurent series
canon.form: On an annulus r < |z - z₀| < R: f(z) = Σ_{n=-∞}^{∞} a_n (z - z₀)^n where a_n = (1/2πi) ∮ f(z)/(z-z₀)^(n+1) dz.

canon.name: Argument principle
canon.form: (1/2πi) ∮_γ f'(z)/f(z) dz = Z - P, where Z = number of zeros and P = number of poles of f inside γ (counted with multiplicity).

subtopics: Arithmetic functions: φ, τ, σ, μ

glossary.definition: a ≡ b (mod n) means n divides (a - b); this is an equivalence relation on ℤ compatible with + and ×.
The count of integers in {1,...,n} coprime to n; φ(n) = n ∏_{p|n} (1 - 1/p).
An integer g whose powers g¹, g², ..., g^{φ(n)} generate all units mod n; exists iff n = 1, 2, 4, p^k, or 2p^k for odd prime p.
An integer a coprime to p is a quadratic residue mod p if a ≡ x² (mod p) has a solution; otherwise a nonresidue.
(a/p) = 1 if a is a QR mod p, -1 if nonresidue, 0 if p|a; computable via Euler's criterion (a/p) ≡ a^((p-1)/2) (mod p).
A function f: ℕ → ℂ; it is multiplicative if gcd(m,n)=1 implies f(mn)=f(m)f(n).
μ(1)=1; μ(n)=(-1)^k if n is a product of k distinct primes; μ(n)=0 if n has a squared prime factor.
A complex number a + bi with a, b ∈ ℤ; forms a UFD Z[i] in which the Euclidean algorithm applies with norm N(a+bi) = a²+b².
n! = n × (n-1) × ... × 2 × 1 for n ≥ 1; 0! = 1. Trailing zeros in n! = ⌊n/5⌋ + ⌊n/25⌋ + ... (Legendre's formula for p=5).
Arithmetic where numbers wrap around at a modulus n. a ≡ b (mod n) means n | (a-b). Used in: Fermat's little theorem, RSA, Chinese Remainder Theorem.
Trailing zeros in n! come from factors of 10 = 2×5. Count = ⌊n/5⌋ + ⌊n/25⌋ + ⌊n/125⌋ + ... (Legendre's formula with p=5). E.g., 100! has ⌊100/5⌋+⌊100/25⌋=20+4=24 trailing zeros.
Trailing zeros in n! = ⌊n/5⌋ + ⌊n/25⌋ + ⌊n/125⌋ + ... (Legendre's formula). Each trailing zero requires one factor of 10 = 2×5; powers of 5 are the limiting factor. Example: 25! has 6 trailing zeros.
a is divisible by b (b|a) if a = kb for some integer k. Tests: div by 2 (even), 3 (digit sum), 5 (ends 0/5), 9 (digit sum). Fundamental theorem: every integer has unique prime factorization.
The largest positive integer dividing both of two integers a and b, written gcd(a,b); computed by the Euclidean algorithm and expressible as ax+by (Bezout's identity).
Two integers are coprime (relatively prime) when their greatest common divisor is 1, i.e. they share no common prime factor.
If gcd(a,n)=1 then a^phi(n) is congruent to 1 modulo n, where phi is Euler's totient function; it generalizes Fermat's little theorem and underlies RSA.
Every integer greater than 1 factors into prime numbers, uniquely up to the order of the factors.

canon.name: Fermat's little theorem
canon.form: If p is prime and p ∤ a, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p); equivalently a^p ≡ a (mod p) for all a.

canon.name: Euler's theorem
canon.form: If gcd(a,n) = 1, then a^{φ(n)} ≡ 1 (mod n); generalizes Fermat's little theorem.

canon.name: Chinese remainder theorem
canon.form: If n₁,...,n_k are pairwise coprime, the system x ≡ a_i (mod n_i) has a unique solution mod n₁...n_k.

canon.name: Wilson's theorem
canon.form: (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p) if and only if p is prime.

canon.name: Quadratic reciprocity
canon.form: For distinct odd primes p, q: (p/q)(q/p) = (-1)^{(p-1)(q-1)/4}; with supplements (−1/p) = (-1)^{(p-1)/2} and (2/p) = (-1)^{(p²-1)/8}.

canon.name: Möbius inversion formula
canon.form: If f(n) = Σ_{d|n} g(d), then g(n) = Σ_{d|n} μ(n/d) f(d).

canon.name: Sum of two squares theorem
canon.form: A positive integer n is a sum of two squares iff in the prime factorization of n, every prime p ≡ 3 (mod 4) occurs to an even power.

canon.name: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions
canon.form: If gcd(a, m) = 1, then the arithmetic progression a, a+m, a+2m,... contains infinitely many primes.

canon.name: Euclidean algorithm for gcd
canon.form: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b); terminates and expresses gcd as integer linear combination (Bezout's identity: gcd(a,b) = sa+tb).

canon.name: Legendre's formula (trailing zeros in n!)
canon.form: The exponent of prime p in n! is Σ_{k=1}^∞ ⌊n/p^k⌋. Trailing zeros in n! = exponent of 5 in n! = ⌊n/5⌋ + ⌊n/25⌋ + ⌊n/125⌋ + ... (more 2s than 5s always).

canon.name: Euler's totient formula
canon.form: φ(n) = n × Π_{p|n} (1 - 1/p). For prime p: φ(p) = p-1. φ(p^k) = p^(k-1)(p-1). φ(mn) = φ(m)φ(n) when gcd(m,n)=1.

canon.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
canon.form: If gcd(m,n)=1, then the system x≡a(mod m), x≡b(mod n) has a unique solution mod mn. Extends to multiple pairwise coprime moduli.

glossary.definition: A relation ≤ on a set that is reflexive (a≤a), antisymmetric (a≤b and b≤a implies a=b), and transitive (a≤b and b≤c implies a≤c).
A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive; partitions the set into equivalence classes.
A pair G = (V, E) of a vertex set V and an edge set E ⊆ V×V; may be directed or undirected, simple or multi.
A connected acyclic graph; a tree on n vertices has exactly n-1 edges.
A graph that can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings.
The minimum number of colors needed to color the vertices of G so that no two adjacent vertices share a color.
A formal power series f(x) = Σ a_n x^n encoding the sequence (a_n); used to solve recurrences and count combinatorial structures.
A permutation with no fixed points; the number of derangements D_n = n! Σ_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k/k! ≈ n!/e.
C_n = (1/(n+1))C(2n,n); counts many combinatorial structures including valid parenthesizations, binary trees, and Dyck paths.
For an equivalence relation ~ on a set S, the equivalence class of a is {x in S : x~a}; the distinct classes partition S into disjoint nonempty subsets.

canon.name: Principle of mathematical induction
canon.form: If P(1) is true and P(k) implies P(k+1) for all k ≥ 1, then P(n) is true for all positive integers n.

canon.name: Strong induction
canon.form: If [P(1) ∧ ... ∧ P(k) ⟹ P(k+1)] for all k, then P(n) holds for all n.

canon.name: Stars and bars (integer compositions)
canon.form: The number of ways to write n as an ordered sum of k non-negative integers is C(n+k-1, k-1).

canon.name: Euler's formula for planar graphs
canon.form: V - E + F = 2 for any connected planar graph with V vertices, E edges, and F faces (including outer face).

canon.name: Four color theorem
canon.form: Every planar graph is 4-colorable (every planar map can be colored with 4 colors so no adjacent regions share a color).

canon.name: Handshaking lemma
canon.form: In any graph, Σ deg(v) = 2|E|; in particular, the number of odd-degree vertices is even.

canon.name: Burnside's lemma (Polya enumeration)
canon.form: The number of distinct orbits under a group G acting on set X is (1/|G|) Σ_{g∈G} |X^g| where X^g = {x : gx = x}.

canon.name: Ramsey's theorem (finite)
canon.form: For any r, s there exists R(r,s) such that any 2-coloring of edges of K_{R(r,s)} contains a red K_r or blue K_s.

subtopics: Topological spaces: open sets, basis, subbasis

glossary.definition: A set X with a topology τ (a collection of open sets) satisfying: ∅ and X are open, arbitrary unions of open sets are open, and finite intersections of open sets are open.
A bijection f: X → Y such that both f and f⁻¹ are continuous; X and Y are topologically identical.
A property preserved by homeomorphism, e.g., compactness, connectedness, Betti numbers.
A topological space that cannot be partitioned into two disjoint nonempty open sets.
A topological space where every open cover has a finite subcover.
A space where any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods; metric spaces are Hausdorff.
A continuous deformation of one continuous map into another; f, g: X→Y are homotopic if there exists H: X×[0,1]→Y with H(x,0)=f(x) and H(x,1)=g(x).
The group of homotopy classes of loops based at x₀ in X, with concatenation as the group operation; measures 1-dimensional 'holes'.
A path-connected space with trivial fundamental group (π₁ = {e}); every loop is contractible to a point.
A space X̃ with a surjection p: X̃ → X such that every point of X has an open neighborhood U whose preimage is a disjoint union of copies of U.
A topological space X is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets; equivalently, the only sets that are both open and closed in X are ∅ and X.
A topological space X is path-connected if for any two points x, y ∈ X there exists a continuous path γ: [0,1] → X with γ(0) = x and γ(1) = y; path-connected implies connected.

canon.name: Tychonoff's theorem
canon.form: An arbitrary product of compact topological spaces is compact (in the product topology).

canon.name: Urysohn's lemma
canon.form: In a normal space (T₄), for any two disjoint closed sets A and B, there exists a continuous f: X → [0,1] with f|_A = 0 and f|_B = 1.

canon.name: Urysohn metrization theorem
canon.form: Every second-countable T₃ (regular Hausdorff) space is metrizable.

canon.name: Seifert-van Kampen theorem
canon.form: If X = U ∪ V with U, V, U∩V open and path-connected, then π₁(X) ≅ π₁(U) *_{π₁(U∩V)} π₁(V) (amalgamated free product).

canon.name: Brouwer fixed-point theorem
canon.form: Every continuous function f: Dⁿ → Dⁿ from the closed n-disk to itself has a fixed point.

canon.name: Classification of compact surfaces
canon.form: Every compact connected surface is homeomorphic to either S², the connected sum of g tori (genus g), or the connected sum of k projective planes.

canon.name: Invariance of domain
canon.form: If U ⊆ ℝⁿ is open and f: U → ℝⁿ is continuous and injective, then f(U) is open and f is a homeomorphism onto f(U).

canon.name: Borsuk-Ulam theorem
canon.form: For every continuous f: Sⁿ → ℝⁿ, there exists x such that f(x) = f(-x); some two antipodal points map to the same point.
